there is a real number $ x $ greater than 1 that is not an integer, and satisfies the following inequality:  
$\{x\}+\left\{x^{3}\right\}+\left\{x^{5}\right\}+\cdots+\left\{x^{99}\right\}<\frac{1}{2^{99}}$  
where $ \{x\} $ denotes the fractional part of $ x $.  
the fractional part of a number is the part after the decimal point, and it is always between 0 (inclusive) and 1 (exclusive).  
for example, if $ x = 2.5 $, then $ \{x\} = 0.5 $.  
the fractional part of a number is periodic and can be used to analyze the behavior of the sum of fractional parts.  
the sum in the inequality involves the fractional parts of $ x $, $ x^3 $, $ x^5 $, up to $ x^{99} $.  
each term in the sum is the fractional part of a power of $ x $.  
the sum is less than $ \frac{1}{2^{99}} $, which is a very small number.  
this suggests that the fractional parts of the powers of $ x $ must be very small.  
since $ x > 1 $, the powers of $ x $ grow rapidly, which means the fractional parts of these powers may become small.  
if $ x $ is chosen such that each fractional part is less than $ \frac{1}{2} $, the sum will be less than $ \frac{1}{2} \times 50 $, which is still larger than $ \frac{1}{2^{99}} $.  
to make the sum smaller, the fractional parts of the powers of $ x $ must be very small.  
this can be achieved if $ x $ is chosen close to an integer, such that its fractional part is very small.  
for example, if $ x = 1.000...0001 $, then $ \{x\} $ is approximately $ 0.000...0001 $, which is very small.  
the sum of the fractional parts would then be very small, and the inequality would be satisfied.
